gm.name: Omar Khan
gm.title: General Manager
gm.previous_role: Vice President of Football and Business Administration, Pittsburgh Steelers

career_path.role: Vice President of Football and Business Administration

scouting_tree.philosophical_lineage: Khan spent 21 years alongside Kevin Colbert, absorbing the Steelers' draft-and-develop ethos. His cap/business background, however, differentiates him. Khan's first hire was bringing in Andy Weidl from the Eagles/Ravens scouting tree, merging the Steelers' traditional toughness-first philosophy with Ozzie Newsome/Howie Roseman-lineage evaluation. Weidl's influence is visible in the heavy trench emphasis and willingness to draft athletes and develop them.
scouting_tree.breakpoints: More aggressive than Colbert on draft-day trades — traded up in round 1 in his very first draft (2023). Also more aggressive in free agency and mid-season trades (DK Metcalf, Jalen Ramsey, Patrick Queen). Willing to make splashy transactions Colbert historically avoided.
scouting_tree.lineage_weight_notes: 3 full drafts of own data, plus Khan has demonstrably diverged from Colbert in trade/FA aggression. Weidl's influence as the actual board-builder is arguably more predictive than Khan's Colbert lineage. Weight will drop further after 2026 draft.

front_office.primary_decision_maker: Omar Khan (final say), with Andy Weidl constructing the draft board

key_influencers.name: Andy Weidl
key_influencers.title: Assistant General Manager
key_influencers.known_biases: Former offensive lineman — loves scouting OL. Schooled under Ozzie Newsome (Ravens) and Howie Roseman (Eagles). Emphasizes physical and mental toughness. Credited as the architect of the OL rebuild (Jones, Fautanu, Frazier, McCormick). Loves linemen who are 'dogs' — physical, high-motor, finishers. May depart for a GM job (interviewed with Falcons, linked to Vikings).

key_influencers.name: Mike McCarthy
key_influencers.title: Head Coach
key_influencers.known_biases: Offensive-minded, West Coast offense background. Wants athletic, mobile QBs who can play within rhythm and extend plays. Committed to keeping 3-4 defense. Values QB development. Known for maximizing QB talent (Favre, Rodgers, Prescott). Will likely push for offensive weapons and pass protection in the draft.

key_influencers.name: Patrick Graham
key_influencers.title: Defensive Coordinator
key_influencers.known_biases: New DC hire under McCarthy. Experience running both 3-4 and 4-3. Ran heavy Cover 3 in Las Vegas in 2025. Will maintain Steelers' 3-4 base. Flexible schematically but values versatile defensive players.

key_influencers.name: Art Rooney II
key_influencers.title: Team President/Owner
key_influencers.known_biases: Values organizational stability and 'the Steeler Way.' Extended Khan through 2028. Does not typically meddle in specific draft picks, but sets philosophical direction. Emphasized maintaining the 3-4 defensive tradition.

front_office.power_dynamics: Khan has final say on selections, but Weidl builds the actual draft board. This is a critical distinction — beat reporters note Weidl, not Khan, is responsible for constructing the team's draft board. Khan's background is in cap management and contract negotiation rather than traditional scouting. McCarthy will have significant input on offensive personnel in his first draft with the team. With Tomlin gone, Khan may now be the primary driving force behind roster decisions.

draft_philosophy.stated_philosophy: Best player available who fits the Steelers' DNA — physical, tough, high-character players
draft_philosophy.actual_philosophy: Leans BPA in round 1 but with a clear positional hierarchy favoring the trenches. In 3 drafts, 3 of 3 first-round picks have been OT or DT. When BPA and trench need align, they pounce. They shift heavily toward need-filling in rounds 2-3 (Frazier at C was a clear need pick; Roman Wilson at WR was a need pick). Day 3 is developmental athletes and 'Steelers DNA' types.
draft_philosophy.philosophy_mismatch_notes: Claims BPA but has taken a trench player with every single first-round pick across 3 drafts. The trench obsession is the actual philosophy, not pure BPA. Khan himself hinted at 2026 combine that the team may finally move away from trenches in round 1 after 3 straight years there.

trade_triggers: A targeted player at a premium position begins to slide toward their pick range (traded up for Broderick Jones in 2023)
Opportunity to accumulate additional Day 2/3 picks by trading back in mid-rounds (traded back in round 3 in 2023 to gain extra 4th rounder)
Mid-season trades for undervalued veterans (Claypool for a 2nd, DK Metcalf for a 2nd, Jalen Ramsey trade)
Reporter Aditi Kinkhabwala noted Khan is 'certainly more aggressive than Kevin Colbert' who only traded up in round 1 three times total
trade_behavior.draft_capital_philosophy: Willing to consolidate and spend picks to move up for targeted players. Also adept at extracting value from outgoing players (Claypool trade was a masterclass). Has sent 2nd-round picks for veterans (Metcalf). More aggressive with draft capital than predecessor Colbert. In 2026, the Steelers project to have 12 picks, giving them ammunition to package picks aggressively.

position_avoidance: QB in round 1 — Khan/Weidl have not drafted a QB in rounds 1-5 in 3 drafts (only Will Howard in round 6 in 2025). Prefer to address QB via trade/FA (Russell Wilson, Justin Fields, Aaron Rodgers). Reported to have 'little if no intention' of taking a QB with their 2026 first-round pick.
RB in early rounds — only drafted Kaleb Johnson in round 3 (2025) after losing Najee Harris in FA. Not a position they invest premium capital in.
position_premium: Offensive line — 4 of first 5 picks in 2024 draft were OL; OT taken in round 1 in both 2023 and 2024. Weidl used 14 of 30 pre-draft visits on OL in 2024.
Interior defensive line — Keeanu Benton (Rd 2, 2023), Logan Lee (Rd 6, 2024), Derrick Harmon (Rd 1, 2025), Yahya Black (Rd 5, 2025). Four DT/DE picks in 3 drafts.
EDGE/OLB — Nick Herbig (Rd 4, 2023), Jack Sawyer (Rd 4, 2025). Essential to 3-4 scheme.

EDGE.archetype_summary: Productive, high-motor, undersized pass rush specialist who fits the 3-4 OLB mold. The Khan/Weidl regime has not invested premium draft capital at EDGE (TJ Watt and Alex Highsmith predate them), instead targeting Day 2-3 productive college rushers who can develop behind established starters. They value sack production and motor over elite length or size.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No hard minimum — drafted Herbig at 31.25 inches, well below typical EDGE thresholds
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: First-step quickness and pass-rush win rate valued over raw speed. Herbig: 4.65 40. Jack Sawyer: 6-4, 260, more traditional size. Speed is secondary to motor and technique.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.70 or faster preferred but not a hard cutoff

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Motor, effort, and relentless pass rush — Herbig led NFL in pass-rush win rate (26.3%) on limited snaps in 2025
most_valued_traits: High motor and effort consistency
Pass rush productivity (sacks, TFLs)
Ability to set the edge in 3-4 scheme
Versatility to stand up or put hand in dirt
Forced fumble/turnover creation
least_important_traits: Elite length — willingness to draft shorter-armed rushers like Herbig shows length is not a priority
Coverage ability — these are rush specialists
deal_breakers: Low college production — both Herbig (11 sacks) and Sawyer were highly productive
Inability to play in a 3-4 scheme
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: High sack production in a Power 5 conference

college_production.production_floor: 9+ sacks or equivalent disruption in final college season for early-round picks
college_production.production_trajectory: Values ascending production — Herbig went from 1 sack to 9 to 11. Sawyer had strip sack in CFP Cotton Bowl as signature play.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Moderate — both Herbig and Sawyer came from strong programs (Wisconsin, Ohio State)

examples: 2023 4.132 — Nick Herbig: 6-2, 240, 4.65 40, 31.25 arms. 11 sacks as junior at Wisconsin. High motor, undersized but productive.
2025 4.123 — Jack Sawyer: 6-4, 260, Ohio State. High-motor DE with run-stopping ability, signature Cotton Bowl strip sack.

EDGE.confidence_note: Only 2 EDGE picks in rounds 1-4 under Khan/Weidl. Both Day 3 selections. The regime has not needed to invest premium capital at EDGE due to having TJ Watt and Alex Highsmith. Profile largely inferred from scheme needs.

OT.archetype_summary: Athletic, long-armed tackles with upside who can move people in the run game. Both OT picks under Khan/Weidl were first-rounders — athletic specimens with 34+ inch arms, strong pass protection traits, and the agility to work in zone-blocking concepts. They want tackles who are finishers with a mean streak.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 34 inches — both Jones (34") and Fautanu (34.5") meet this threshold
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: 40-yard dash (both sub-5.01), explosiveness metrics. Jones: 4.97 40 (92nd percentile for OT), 30" vert. Fautanu: 5.01 40, 32.5" vert, 113" broad jump.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Sub-5.05 40 preferred — values athletic OT who can reach the second level

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Athletic pass protectors who can also reach-block and climb to the second level in the run game
most_valued_traits: Athleticism and foot speed for pass protection
Length usage and hand placement
Finishing mentality — 'mean streak'
Agility to work in zone concepts
High college-level pass blocking grades
least_important_traits: Pure mauling power — both picks were athletes first, power blockers second
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite athleticism score for the position — both Jones and Fautanu had 88th+ percentile speed scores for OT

college_production.production_floor: Must be multi-year starter at a Power 5 school. Jones: started every game in 2022 for Georgia, zero sacks allowed. Fautanu: started every game for 2 years at Washington, won Morris Trophy.
college_production.production_trajectory: Values ascending arc — both were redshirts who developed into All-Conference starters
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — both came from elite programs (Georgia, Washington)

examples: 2023 1.14 — Broderick Jones: 6-5, 311, 4.97 40, 34" arms. Georgia. Zero sacks allowed in 2022. Traded UP to select him. Khan: 'Love his athleticism. Love his upside.'
2024 1.20 — Troy Fautanu: 6-4, 317, 5.01 40, 34.5" arms. Washington. Won Morris Trophy, Joe Moore Award. Khan: 'He was the player we wanted.'

IOL.archetype_summary: Tough, physical, high-character interior linemen who are plug-and-play starters. Values toughness and wrestling background (Frazier), positional versatility, and the ability to anchor. Not looking for pure athletes — looking for technicians who play with a nasty edge and can start immediately.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No hard minimum — Frazier at 32.25" arms is below typical thresholds but excels technically
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Bench press (Frazier: 30 reps) and short shuttle valued. Anchor strength matters more than straight-line speed.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Not a priority — Frazier's 5.24 40 is slow but fine for IOL

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Anchor strength, hand fighting, and finishing ability at the point of attack
most_valued_traits: Toughness and physicality — Frazier bear-crawled off field with broken fibula
Mauling run blocking with leg drive
Multi-year starting experience
Positional versatility (McCormick can play guard; Anderson is versatile)
Wrestling/combat sports background valued
least_important_traits: Raw athleticism — they've taken technically sound, tough players over athletic freaks
deal_breakers: Injury-prone history (though Frazier was taken despite broken fibula)
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite college production metrics (McCormick had 90.3 PFF grade, best among draft-eligible guards)

college_production.production_floor: Must be multi-year starter with All-Conference honors. Frazier: 4-year starter, 2x All-American, 3x team captain. McCormick: school-record 57 consecutive starts.
college_production.production_trajectory: Values durability and consistency — both had extended starting runs
college_production.context_sensitivity: Willing to draft from small schools (McCormick from South Dakota State)

examples: 2024 2.51 — Zach Frazier: 6-3, 313, 32.25" arms. West Virginia C. 2x All-American. Wrestling background. PFWA All-Rookie Team.
2024 4.119 — Mason McCormick: 6-4, 308. South Dakota State G. School-record 57 consecutive starts. 90.3 PFF grade.
2023 7.251 — Spencer Anderson: Maryland OL. Versatile interior player, developmental pick.

DT.archetype_summary: Big, long, powerful interior defenders with versatility to play multiple techniques in the 3-4 front. The Steelers consistently target DTs with 34+ inch arms, 300+ pounds, and the ability to two-gap AND penetrate. They want players who can be disruptive against both the run and pass, with size/length to fill gaps and the athleticism to generate interior pressure.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 34 inches — Harmon (34.375"), Benton (likely similar). The Steelers explicitly noted Harmon's 34+ inch arms as ideal.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: 40-yard dash matters for DT (Harmon: 4.95, Benton: 5.09). 10-yard split important for first-step burst. Values combination of mass AND movement.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Versatility to play 3-technique, 2i-technique, or nose in the 3-4 front. Must be stout against the run AND generate interior pressure.
most_valued_traits: Length and wingspan for controlling blockers
Upper body strength and powerful hands
Run defense versatility — can two-gap or penetrate
Interior pass rush ability (Harmon led FBS DTs with 55 pressures in 2024)
Motor and high snap count tolerance (Benton played 80-85% of snaps)
least_important_traits: Pure finesse pass-rush moves — these are power-first defenders
deal_breakers: Cannot hold the point of attack against double teams
Limited positional versatility — must play multiple alignments in 3-4
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Rare combination of size, length, and pass-rush production from the interior

college_production.production_floor: 4+ sacks or 10+ TFLs for early-round DT. Harmon: 5 sacks, 10.5 TFLs. Benton: 4.5 sacks, 10 TFLs.
college_production.production_trajectory: Open to transfer portal risers — Harmon transferred from Michigan State to Oregon and exploded. Benton was a steady multi-year producer at Wisconsin.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Adjusts for scheme — Harmon's breakout was partly attributed to Oregon's scheme unleashing him

examples: 2025 1.21 — Derrick Harmon: 6-4.5, 313, 4.95 40, 34.375" arms, 83" wingspan. Oregon. 5 sacks, 10.5 TFLs, 55 pressures. Tomlin: 'He has Steelers DNA.'
2023 2.49 — Keeanu Benton: 6-4, 309, 5.09 40, 7.34 3-cone. Wisconsin. 4.5 sacks, 10 TFLs. 'Big strong guy. Really active. Good hands.'
2024 6.178 — Logan Lee: Iowa DL. Run defense specialist. Ranked 5th in Big Ten DT run-defense grade.
2025 5.156 — Yahya Black: Iowa DL. Versatile 4-technique. Run defense focus.

CB.archetype_summary: Long, physical, press-man corners with length, size, and physicality at the catch point. Joey Porter Jr. is the prototype — 6-2+, 34" arms, physical in press coverage, disrupts at the catch point. They value length and press technique over pure speed or ball production. Perfect for a man-heavy scheme that relies on blitzing.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No hard floor but strong preference for 32+ inches. Porter Jr: 34" arms. Ryan Watts: 6-2 with physicality. Cory Trice: 6-3.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed matters but is not the primary filter. Porter Jr: 4.46 40. Values length metrics (arm length, wingspan) over pure timed speed.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.50 or faster preferred but will go slower for elite length/physicality

playing_style.primary_coverage_style: Press-man — the Steelers' blitz-heavy scheme demands corners who can hold up one-on-one
most_valued_traits: Length and disruption at the catch point
Press technique and physicality at the line
Run support and willingness to tackle
Hip fluidity and transition in man coverage
Competitive toughness — Porter Jr was called 'most physical CB in the draft class'
least_important_traits: Interception production — Porter Jr had limited INT numbers but elite PBUs (11 in final season)
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite length combined with press technique — Porter Jr's 34" arms are freakish for a CB

college_production.production_floor: No clear statistical floor — Porter Jr had only 2 INTs in college. Values PBUs and film over raw INT numbers.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers ascending producers — Porter Jr went from 4 PBUs to 11 in breakout junior year
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — adjusts for competition level faced

examples: 2023 2.32 — Joey Porter Jr.: 6-2.5, 193, 4.46 40, 34" arms. Penn State. 11 PBUs as junior. Physical press-man specialist. Legacy pick but also BPA.
2023 7.241 — Cory Trice Jr.: 6-3, Purdue. Physically imposing, aggressive at the LOS. Day 3 flier on size/length.
2024 6.195 — Ryan Watts: 6-2, Texas. 84.9 run-defense grade. Physical corner with tackling prowess.

S.archetype_summary: The Khan/Weidl regime has not drafted a safety in rounds 1-4 across 3 drafts. Safety has been addressed through trades (Minkah Fitzpatrick predated them; traded for Jalen Ramsey in 2025) and free agency. Based on Steelers' defensive scheme and scouting tree, they likely value versatile safeties who can play single-high, come down into the box, and support against the run in the AFC North.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No data — no safeties drafted in early rounds
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Range (40 time, vertical) and change-of-direction (3-cone) would be inferred priorities based on single-high demands
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.50 or faster likely required for a single-high role

playing_style.primary_coverage_style: Versatile — must play both single-high and in the box. The Steelers' blitz-heavy scheme demands safeties who can cover when extra rushers are sent.
most_valued_traits: Range and ability to cover ground from a deep alignment
Physicality and willingness to support against the run
Versatility to play multiple safety roles
Tackling reliability — non-negotiable in AFC North
least_important_traits: Slot coverage ability — typically use nickel corners for that role

college_production.production_floor: No data — no early-round S picks under this regime

S.confidence_note: Zero safeties drafted in rounds 1-4 across 3 drafts. Profile entirely inferred from scheme needs and scouting tree. Weidl's Ravens background valued versatile safeties (Ed Reed, Matt Elam types). The Steelers have addressed S through veteran acquisitions (Fitzpatrick, Ramsey).

LB.archetype_summary: Length, athleticism, and coverage ability in a do-it-all linebacker who can blitz, cover, and tackle sideline-to-sideline. Payton Wilson is the prototype — 6-4, 242 lbs, elite coverage grades, first-round talent who fell due to medical concerns. The regime is willing to bet on upside and medical risk at LB when the talent justifies it.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No hard threshold but values length — Payton Wilson at 6-4 is among the longest LBs in his class
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Coverage athleticism is paramount. Payton Wilson earned 90.4 coverage grade at NC State. Speed and range valued.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.55 or faster preferred to cover in the modern passing game

playing_style.primary_coverage_style: Three-down linebacker who can cover tight ends and running backs
most_valued_traits: Coverage ability — Payton Wilson's 90.4 coverage grade was elite
Sideline-to-sideline range and tackling volume (138 tackles)
Blitz ability (6 sacks for Payton Wilson)
Length and intelligence
Ball-hawking ability (3 INTs for Payton Wilson)
least_important_traits: Pure thumping/run-stuffing ability without coverage skills
deal_breakers: Cannot cover in space — the modern NFL requires LBs to match up in passing game
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite coverage grades combined with blitz production — will take medical risks for this profile

college_production.production_floor: Must be a high-volume tackler with coverage production. Payton Wilson: 138 tackles, 6 sacks, 3 INTs.
college_production.production_trajectory: Willing to bet on late breakouts if the athletic profile is elite
college_production.context_sensitivity: Moderate — values production but medical evaluation clearly a major factor (Payton Wilson's missing ACL was known)

examples: 2024 3.98 — Payton Wilson: 6-4, 242. NC State. 138 tackles, 18.5 TFLs, 6 sacks, 3 INTs. 89.9 PFF grade, 90.4 coverage grade. Fell due to missing ACL in one knee. First-round talent at third-round price.
2025 7.226 — Carson Bruener: Washington LB. 104 tackles, 3 INTs. Son of former Steelers TE Mark Bruener. Special teams contributor profile.
Note: Nick Herbig (4.132, 2023) plays OLB/EDGE, covered under EDGE section.

WR.archetype_summary: The regime has invested minimal draft capital at WR — only one pick in rounds 1-4 across 3 drafts (Roman Wilson, Rd 3, 2024). That pick was a slot/versatile receiver who is a willing blocker. The regime has preferred to address WR through trades (DK Metcalf, George Pickens was a Colbert pick). When they do draft a WR, they want a complementary piece, not a true alpha receiver. Willing blockers with versatility are preferred.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No data — only one WR drafted in early rounds
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Route running and slot versatility valued over raw speed or size

playing_style.primary_role: Slot-capable, versatile receiver who can block and play multiple roles
most_valued_traits: Willingness to block — Roman Wilson was noted as 'a willing blocker'
Versatility to play multiple receiver positions
Big-game production (Wilson caught TDs in Michigan wins over Ohio State and Alabama)
Route running and separation ability
least_important_traits: Contested catch ability — they acquire that via trade (Pickens, Metcalf)
Deep threat speed — not the primary target of drafted WRs
deal_breakers: Cannot block — the Steelers' run-first identity demands WRs who engage
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: No clear pattern from such limited data

college_production.production_floor: No clear threshold — Roman Wilson had 12 TDs but was a complementary piece at Michigan
college_production.production_trajectory: Unknown with only one data point
college_production.context_sensitivity: Wilson came from a run-heavy national championship team, so production was contextually strong

examples: 2024 3.84 — Roman Wilson: 5-11, 189. Michigan. 12 TDs in championship season. Slot/versatile profile. Willing blocker. Missed most of rookie season with ankle injury.

WR.confidence_note: Only 1 WR drafted in rounds 1-4 across 3 drafts. The regime clearly prefers to acquire WR1/WR2 types via trade (DK Metcalf for 2nd-round pick, George Pickens inherited from Colbert). Draft WR picks are role players and complementary pieces. McCarthy's WR-heavy offensive philosophy may shift this in 2026.

TE.archetype_summary: Massive, physically dominant blocking tight end who is a mismatch weapon due to sheer size. Darnell Washington is the archetype — 6-7, 264 (combine weight, reportedly plays at 280-315), 4.64 40-yard dash. The Steelers want a tight end who can be an extra offensive lineman in the run game while also being too big for defenders to cover in the red zone.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No specific threshold but extreme size is the differentiator
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: 40-yard dash matters relative to size — Washington ran 4.64 at 272 lbs (combine). Broad jump important for explosiveness (Washington: 10-2).
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.70 or faster at 260+ lbs — must be athletic for size

playing_style.primary_role: Blocking-first TE who provides a mismatch in the passing game due to size
most_valued_traits: Elite inline blocking ability — Washington was considered best blocking TE in 2023 class
Freakish size/speed combination
Red zone threat due to sheer physical dominance
Run game enhancement — essentially a 6th offensive lineman
Durability
least_important_traits: Route running refinement — size IS the route-running tool
Receiving volume — Washington had only 45 catches in 3 years at Georgia
deal_breakers: Cannot block — the Steelers already had Pat Freiermuth for receiving
Undersized for the blocking role they need
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Rare size/athleticism combination that doesn't exist at other positions

college_production.production_floor: Low receiving production is acceptable if blocking is elite. Washington: 45 catches for 774 yards in 3 years.
college_production.production_trajectory: Not applicable — blocking value is the primary metric
college_production.context_sensitivity: Washington came from Georgia's loaded offense, limiting volume

examples: 2023 3.93 — Darnell Washington: 6-7, 272 (combine), 4.64 40. Georgia. 'Mount Washington.' Russell Wilson says he plays at 315 lbs. Top-5 TE run-blocking grade in 2022. Khan traded back to get him plus an extra 4th-round pick.

TE.confidence_note: Only 1 TE drafted in rounds 1-4 across 3 drafts. The Steelers already had Pat Freiermuth (pre-Khan) as their receiving TE, so Washington filled a specific need. McCarthy's offense may use more 11-personnel (3 WR) than 12-personnel (2 TE), potentially changing TE draft approach.

RB.archetype_summary: Physical, between-the-tackles runner who fits the 'Steelers DNA' ethos of toughness. Only one RB drafted across 3 drafts (Kaleb Johnson, Rd 3, 2025), taken to replace Najee Harris after he left in free agency. Not a position they invest premium capital in — Johnson at pick 83 is the highest they've gone. Prefer bigger backs who can handle the physical AFC North style.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Size and physicality matter more than timed speed
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Not a primary concern — values physicality over speed

playing_style.primary_role: Physical, between-the-tackles early-down back who can handle a high workload
most_valued_traits: Physical running style — Johnson at 6-1, 224 is a physical back
Ability to handle cold-weather AFC North football
Durability to be a lead back
Complementary to smaller, faster backs already on roster (Jaylen Warren, Kenneth Gainwell)
least_important_traits: Receiving ability — not the primary role
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: No data — they don't reach for RBs

college_production.production_floor: Must be a productive college runner. Johnson was Iowa's lead back.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Johnson came from Iowa's run-heavy system

examples: 2025 3.83 — Kaleb Johnson: 6-1, 224. Iowa. Physical runner who 'seamlessly fills the shoes' of Najee Harris per ESPN. Bigger than Warren and Gainwell.

RB.confidence_note: Only 1 RB drafted across 3 drafts. The regime clearly deprioritizes early RB investment. Najee Harris (Rd 1, 2021) was a Colbert pick.

QB.archetype_summary: The Khan regime has pointedly avoided investing premium draft capital in quarterback. Kenny Pickett (Rd 1, 2022) was the last Colbert draft; Khan traded him to the Eagles. The regime has preferred veteran acquisitions (Russell Wilson, Justin Fields, Aaron Rodgers) and took only a Day 3 flier on Will Howard (Rd 6, 2025). When Khan does describe what they want, he emphasizes toughness to handle the AFC North — cold weather, physicality, rivalries. McCarthy wants an athletic passer who can play within rhythm and extend plays.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Arm strength and pocket mobility valued. McCarthy: 'You want a more athletic player in that position, the man that can extend plays.'
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Not a running QB preference but must have enough mobility to escape and extend

playing_style.primary_style: Pocket passer with mobility to extend plays, play within rhythm and timing of West Coast concepts
most_valued_traits: Toughness to handle AFC North physicality and cold weather
Ability to process quickly within West Coast timing concepts
Arm strength for cold-weather division play
Leadership and winning pedigree (Howard won national championship)
Development potential to be a 10-15 year starter
least_important_traits: Designed rushing ability — not looking for a run-first QB
deal_breakers: Cannot handle physical AFC North play
Khan: 'We have to find someone who can handle it'
Poor accuracy in short-to-intermediate game (McCarthy's system demands it)
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Khan views only Fernando Mendoza as a first-round QB in 2026 — suggests very high bar for round 1 QB investment

college_production.production_floor: Must have starting experience and winning pedigree. Howard: led Ohio State to national championship.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Heavy emphasis on program quality and competition level

examples: 2025 6.185 — Will Howard: 6-4, 235. Ohio State. National championship winner. 13 consecutive completions in title game, 231 yards, 2 TDs, 0 INT. Sixth-round developmental pick. Khan praises his development. Missed entire 2025 season with hand injury.

QB.confidence_note: Only 1 QB drafted (Day 3). The regime has avoided premium QB investment, preferring veterans. McCarthy's hiring may shift this — he's a 'renowned developer of quarterbacks.' The Steelers reportedly have 'little if no intention' of taking a QB in round 1 of the 2026 draft. They may target a QB in Day 2 only if they believe the ceiling exceeds Will Howard's.

reach_tolerance.notes: Generally has drafted at or near consensus value. The trade up for Jones (from 17 to 14) was modest and Jones was projected in the 12-18 range. Fautanu at 20 was consensus value. Harmon at 21 was slightly ahead of some boards (Brugler had him 19th). The regime does not make wild reaches — Weidl's Ravens/Eagles background emphasizes board discipline.

scheme_fit.offensive_scheme: West Coast offense under McCarthy, evolving into what he calls a 'power spread' — horizontal passing game with play-action off the run game. More shotgun, quick passing, and QB freedom than the Arthur Smith/Matt Canada systems. Values athletic, versatile OL who can work in zone-blocking concepts. WR-heavy formations expected to increase.
scheme_fit.defensive_scheme: 3-4 base defense maintained under new DC Patrick Graham. Has been the Steelers' scheme since 1992. Blitz-heavy, relies on elite OLBs (TJ Watt, Alex Highsmith, Nick Herbig). Graham expected to add more Cover 3 elements. Needs versatile DL who can play multiple techniques and LBs who can cover.
scheme_fit.scheme_change_notes: MAJOR coaching change: Mike Tomlin resigned January 2026 after 19 years. Mike McCarthy hired as replacement January 27, 2026. Entire coaching staff replaced. Offensive scheme will change significantly (West Coast concepts replacing Arthur Smith's run-heavy approach). Defensive scheme retaining 3-4 base but new DC Patrick Graham will add his own wrinkles. Draft tendencies on offense may shift toward WR and QB given McCarthy's preferences.

psychological_profile.risk_description: Moderately aggressive. Willing to take calculated risks — traded up for Jones in his first draft, traded 2nd-round pick for DK Metcalf, took Payton Wilson despite missing ACL. But also takes safe, high-floor picks at other positions (Frazier, McCormick). Generally 1 high-upside bet per draft with 'safer' picks elsewhere.
psychological_profile.recency_description: Moderate. The 2025 draft (Harmon, Johnson, Sawyer, Black) was heavily influenced by the Steelers' disastrous final game — a 30-6 playoff loss where the Ravens ran for 299 yards on 50 carries. The run defense emphasis was a direct response.
psychological_profile.sunk_cost_behavior: Moves on quickly. Traded Kenny Pickett (1st-round QB pick from Colbert's final draft) to the Eagles after just 2 seasons. Did not double-down on the investment. Traded George Pickens (Colbert's 2nd-round WR) to Dallas when the relationship soured. Decisive and unsentimental about roster moves.
psychological_profile.combine_description: Balanced but leans toward valuing measurables at certain positions. Clearly values OT athleticism (both Jones and Fautanu had elite athletic scores for the position). At DT, values the size/length/speed combination. At CB, values length over speed. But has also drafted players with modest testing who excelled on film (Frazier didn't run at the combine, McCormick from a small school).
psychological_profile.character_description: High character emphasis. Khan repeatedly uses the phrase 'Steelers DNA' to describe the types of players they target. Tomlin (and now McCarthy) emphasize physical and mental toughness. Weidl has talked about 'physical and mental toughness' throughout his career. The regime avoids character risks — no notable character-concern picks in 3 drafts.
psychological_profile.quarterback_philosophy: Avoids premium QB investment in the draft. Prefers veteran solutions (Wilson, Fields, Rodgers) and developmental Day 3 picks (Howard). Khan: 'Like all the fans, we're all searching for the next franchise player, the guy that's going to be our quarterback for 10 to 15 years.' But has not been willing to trade up or use a 1st-round pick on QB in his tenure. The only 1st-round QB under his watch was Pickett (Colbert's pick), whom Khan traded away. With McCarthy's QB development reputation, this may evolve.

college_production_preferences.production_vs_projection: Leans toward productive college players but will bet on projection at premium positions when the athletic profile is elite. Jones was a high-floor/high-ceiling pick. The regime generally avoids pure projection bets — even their 'developmental' picks (Herbig, McCormick) were highly productive in college.

production_thresholds_by_position.OT: Must be a multi-year starter at a Power 5 program with All-Conference honors
production_thresholds_by_position.DT: Minimum 4 sacks or 10 TFLs in final college season for rounds 1-2
production_thresholds_by_position.CB: No clear statistical floor — values film and PBUs over interception totals
production_thresholds_by_position.IOL: Must be a multi-year starter with All-Conference recognition
production_thresholds_by_position.LB: High tackle volume required — Payton Wilson had 138 tackles
production_thresholds_by_position.WR: Only one WR drafted — insufficient data for thresholds

college_production_preferences.notes: The regime values proven college production more than breakout age or dominator ratings. Multi-year starters and team captains (Frazier: 3x captain) are preferred. However, they accepted Harmon's one-year transfer breakout at Oregon, showing flexibility when the film and measurables support it.

IOL.notes: Values toughness and technique over athletic testing. Frazier didn't even run the 40 at the combine.

EDGE.notes: Drafted Herbig with 31.25 inch arms, breaking typical EDGE length thresholds. Production matters more than measurables.

measurable_thresholds.threshold_type: Hard cutoffs at OT (arm length, athleticism) and DT (arm length, size). More flexible at other positions where production and film carry more weight.

size_preferences_by_position.OT: 310+ lbs with 34+ inch arms — both picks met this exactly
size_preferences_by_position.DT: 305+ lbs with 34+ inch arms in 3-4 scheme
size_preferences_by_position.CB: Prefers 6-0+ with length but has drafted smaller Day 3 CBs (Donte Kent: 5-10)
size_preferences_by_position.TE: Extreme size preference — 6-5+ and 260+ for blocking TEs

measurable_thresholds.athletic_testing_favorites: 40-yard dash relative to size is valued at OT and DT (looking for 'athletes' at big-man positions). Bench press matters for IOL (Frazier: 30 reps). At CB, 40 time matters less than length metrics. Vertical jump and broad jump are secondary across positions.
measurable_thresholds.measurables_vs_film: Film-first at most positions, but measurables serve as eliminators at OT and DT. The 34-inch arm length threshold at tackle and DT appears to be a hard line. At other positions, film and production outweigh testing — Frazier was drafted despite not running the 40 at the combine.

round_strategy.round_1_strategy: Premium trench players — 3 consecutive first-round picks have been OT or DT. Willing to trade up modestly (3 spots for Jones). Targets the highest-rated player at a position of need, with a clear preference for the trenches. Khan hinted at 2026 combine that they may move away from trenches in round 1 for the first time, pivoting toward WR or other offensive positions.
round_strategy.round_2_3_strategy: Fills remaining premium needs. Round 2 picks have been CB (Porter Jr.) and C (Frazier) — high-value contributors at need positions. Round 3 picks address secondary needs (Washington at TE, Roman Wilson at WR, Kaleb Johnson at RB). Willing to trade back in this range to accumulate picks (traded back in round 3 in 2023 to get extra 4th).
round_strategy.day_3_strategy: Productive college players who embody 'Steelers DNA' — tough, physical, high-character. Day 3 picks include developmental athletes (Herbig, McCormick, Payton Wilson at compensatory 3rd) and legacy/connection picks (Carson Bruener, son of former Steeler). Special teams contributors and positional depth. Late-round QB developmental pick (Howard). Willing to take medical risks on Day 3 (Cory Trice had injury history).
round_strategy.compensatory_pick_usage: Have not received compensatory picks in recent years due to FA acquisition patterns. When they've had extra picks via trade (the Claypool-derived 2nd rounder), they've used them on premium talents (Porter Jr.).

OT.note: Jones is starting RT; Fautanu lost rookie season to injury but expected to start in 2025. Both are long-term starters if healthy.

DT.note: Benton is a full-time starter, Harmon projected as immediate starter. Lee and Black are depth pieces. Strong evaluation track record.

IOL.note: Frazier earned PFWA All-Rookie Team and Joe Greene Award. McCormick started as a rookie. Anderson is depth. Excellent hit rate.

CB.note: Porter Jr. is a full-time starter. Trice and Watts are depth pieces. Porter Jr. alone justifies the position investment.

EDGE.note: Herbig has been highly productive on limited snaps (NFL-best 26.3% pass-rush win rate, 7.5 sacks in 2025). Sawyer is a rookie.

LB.note: Payton Wilson earned significant playing time as a rookie, made key plays (INT vs Ravens, fumble recovery TD vs Bengals). Bruener is a special teamer.

WR.note: Roman Wilson missed most of rookie season with injury. Too early to evaluate but concerning that only 1 WR has been drafted.

TE.note: Washington has carved out a significant role as blocking TE and red zone target.

RB.note: Johnson is a 2025 rookie — too early to evaluate.

QB.note: Howard missed entire 2025 season with hand injury. Developmental pick, too early to evaluate.

blind_spots: WR evaluation is a concern — only 1 WR drafted in 3 years, and that pick (Roman Wilson) has not yet contributed significantly
QB evaluation — traded away Colbert's Pickett pick, have avoided premium QB investment entirely
strengths: Offensive line evaluation is elite — Frazier, Jones, McCormick, Fautanu represent a complete OL rebuild via the draft
Interior defensive line evaluation is strong — Benton and Harmon are high-quality starters
Day 2/3 value extraction — Herbig (Rd 4) and Payton Wilson (comp Rd 3) are above-draft-slot contributors

free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_spending_philosophy: Increasingly aggressive. Khan has been described as having 'utilized an aggressive approach in free agency.' Signed Isaac Seumalo (3-year, $24M for G), Patrick Queen (LB), Darius Slay (CB), Aaron Rodgers ($13.6M). Traded for DK Metcalf (gave up 2nd-round pick), Jalen Ramsey, Jonnu Smith. Willing to give up draft capital and spend money to fill needs, then still draft BPA. Khan at 2026 combine: 'We have the flexibility to maybe do some things that might not be normal.'
free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_draft_correlation: When a position is addressed aggressively in FA/trade, it drops as a draft priority. After trading for Metcalf in 2025, the Steelers did not draft a WR that year. After signing Rodgers, they only drafted a 6th-round QB. When a veteran starter leaves (Najee Harris to Chargers), they draft a replacement (Kaleb Johnson). There IS double-dipping at the trenches — they signed Seumalo AND drafted multiple OL.
free_agency_draft_interaction.roster_construction_philosophy: Build through the draft at premium positions (OL, DL, EDGE, CB, LB), supplement through FA and trades at positions where immediate help is needed (QB, WR, S). Khan is increasingly willing to be aggressive in FA/trades compared to predecessor Colbert, but the draft remains the primary talent acquisition vehicle. With 12 projected picks in 2026 and cap space, the regime has maximum flexibility.

notable_patterns: 3 consecutive first-round picks on the trenches (OT-OT-DT) — unprecedented positional concentration
10 of 21 picks (48%) have been offensive or defensive linemen — building from the inside out
Only 1 WR and 1 QB drafted across 3 drafts — premium offensive skill positions addressed via trade/FA
Heavy Big Ten pipeline — 2025 draft was nearly all-Big Ten (6 of 7 picks from Big Ten schools)
Khan traded up in round 1 in his very first draft (2023) — more aggressive than predecessor Colbert
Weidl used 14 of 30 pre-draft visits on OL in 2024, confirming the trench obsession

biggest_reaches.context: Modest reach if any — Brugler had Harmon 19th overall. Clear need after run defense collapse in playoff loss (Ravens ran for 299 yards).

biggest_steals.context: First-round talent who fell to compensatory 3rd due to medical concerns (missing ACL in one knee). PFF: 'If it weren't for that recent news, this would be an elite grade.'
Expected first-round pick who fell to early 2nd round. Steelers had the pick via the Chase Claypool trade (Khan's shrewd mid-season deal with Bears).

confidence_notes: 3 drafts of data as Steelers GM. Khan's background is in cap management and business administration rather than traditional scouting — Andy Weidl (assistant GM) is the actual board-builder, making Weidl's potential departure to a GM job a significant variable. Additionally, the coaching change from Tomlin to McCarthy represents a major regime shift that could alter draft approach, particularly on offense. The 2026 draft will be revealing — Khan hinted at moving away from trenches in round 1 for the first time. Profile should be refreshed after the 2026 NFL Draft.
